forecasts.reasoning:
The question asks whether an independence referendum for Alberta will be officially scheduled before July 1, 2025 (within the next 54 days from the knowledge cutoff of May 7, 2025). 

The UCP government, led by Premier Danielle Smith, recently tabled Bill 54 to lower the signature threshold for citizen-initiated referendums from 20% of eligible voters (~600,000) to 10% of voters in the last general election (~177,000) and extend the collection period to 120 days. Premier Smith explicitly announced on May 5, 2025, that her government does not support separation and will *not* initiate an independence referendum themselves. Instead, they will place the question on a 2026 ballot only if a citizen-led petition successfully gathers the required signatures under the new legislation.

Given this stated policy, the government will not proactively schedule the referendum. The only path to the referendum being officially scheduled is through the citizen initiative process. Within the 54 days before July 1, 2025, it is practically impossible for the entire required sequence to occur: Bill 54 must receive Royal Assent, proponents must have their petition approved, they must collect 177,000 signatures, and Elections Alberta must verify all signatures before the government officially schedules the vote. Because this administrative and signature-gathering process will take several months at a minimum, the referendum cannot be officially scheduled before July 1, 2025. I assign a baseline 5% chance to a "black swan" political crisis that somehow causes the premier to change her stance and unilaterally schedule a snap referendum.

The question asks if Starlink's website will show Lesotho as a country with Starlink availability before July 1, 2025.
As of mid-April 2025 (specifically April 14-16, 2025), Lesotho Communications Authority granted Starlink a 10-year operating license.
Starlink’s availability map currently says 'Starting in 2025' for Lesotho.
Historically, looking at other African countries:
- Zimbabwe: License granted May 2024, launched September 2024 (about 3.5 months).
- Botswana: License granted May 2024, launched August 2024 (about 3 months).
- Madagascar: Regulatory agreements around April/May 2024, launched end of June 2024 (about 2 months).

The time between license approval and official launch (when it becomes "Available" on the map) in neighboring and functionally similar countries has consistently been around 2 to 4 months. Lesotho's license was granted on April 14, 2025. 
A 2.5 month window brings us to early July. This makes it slightly tight, but historically they have occasionally moved faster (Madagascar was ~2 months). 
However, Starlink's map update for 'Available' happens as soon as commercial service launches. 
Given the knowledge cutoff is May 2025, the launch is very recent or imminent. 
If it takes exactly 2.5 months from April 14, it would be end of June 2025. There's a decent chance the map updates just before July 1, 2025. We know Starlink has already started updating its map to show 2025 for Lesotho and moving aggressively in Africa.
However, considering typical bureaucratic or logistical delays, there's some chance it spills out to July or August 2025. But given Elon Musk’s recent meeting with Lesotho's PM (Sept 2024) and the fact that they have a local entity ready ("Starlink Lesotho"), the infrastructure setup (e.g. gateways in neighboring countries or purely satellite-to-dish) shouldn't take too long.

I will predict 80% because standard launch timeframes post-license approval in the southern Africa region (Madagascar, Botswana, Zimbabwe) suggest a 2 to 3.5 month turnaround, and reaching July 1st gives them exactly 2.5 months. It's a bit on the edge, but leaning towards a high probability of map updating to "Available" since they are eager to expand, though there's a 20% risk of typical roll-out delays pushing it past July 1.

The recall involves a substantial amount of product (over 212,000 pounds of liquid egg products) distributed broadly, which complicates the retrieval and disposal process. Recalls involving the FSIS require the firm to complete all reasonable efforts to retrieve and dispose of the product and require FSIS to complete extensive effectiveness checks before issuing a Recall Closure Notice. It is very common for government agencies, including FSIS, to take many months—often 6 to 12 months—to officially mark a large-scale recall as 'Closed' due to the administrative requirements and thoroughness of the effectiveness audits. Given that the recall occurred on March 28, 2025, a deadline of July 1, 2025, leaves only about three months for the process to be fully completed and for the website's status to reflect the change, which is typically insufficient time. Therefore, the probability is quite low.

The forecast window is narrow, spanning only 34 days from May 12 to June 15, 2025. As of early May 2025, leader-level communication between Trump and Xi appears stalled. While they held an officially acknowledged call in January shortly before Trump's inauguration, recent attempts at communication have been clouded by diplomatic discord. In late April, Trump claimed a phone call had taken place regarding tariffs, which China explicitly denied. The resolution criteria specifically require both nations to acknowledge the call, meaning unilateral claims by Trump will not suffice.

Additionally, while there have been reports of a potential "birthday summit" in mid-June, Chinese officials have expressed wariness, preferring deeply scripted encounters and demanding "ground rules" be established by lower-level officials first. It was reported in late March that agreeing to a meeting could take "months." If the summit is delayed, a preparatory phone call is also likely to be pushed back. Even if the summit proceeds, standard diplomatic practice could see envoys handling the groundwork without the leaders needing to speak on the phone right before the in-person meeting. Given China's demonstrated willingness to publicly deny unscripted contact and the slow pace of diplomatic alignment, the likelihood of a mutually acknowledged phone call within this specific short window is assessed to be moderately low.

A massive anti-government protest (following the arrest of Ekrem İmamoğlu) started in Turkey on March 19, 2025, with an estimated 300,000 participants. This protest perfectly matches the inclusion criteria for Carnegie's Global Protest Tracker (an anti-government protest surpassing 10,000 participants). The only uncertainty is whether Carnegie's database will be updated to include it by the July 1, 2025 resolution date. Snippets suggest the tracker was last updated in November 2024, meaning it had not been updated for about six months as of May 2025. Given that it typically receives periodic updates (likely semi-annually or a few times a year), there is a coin-flip to slightly favorable chance that a scheduled update occurs and captures the Turkish protests before the July 1 cutoff.
